title: Geometry and Topology of Spectral Minimal Partitions
speaker: Graham Cox
abstract:
A minimal partition is a decomposition of a manifold into disjoint sets that minimizes a spectral energy functional. In the bipartite case minimal partitions are closely related to eigenfunctions of the Laplacian, but in the non-bipartite case they are difficult to classify, even for simple domains like the square or the circle.
I will present new results that imply, among other things, that a partition that minimizes energy locally is in fact a global minimum (in the bipartite case) and a minimum within a certain topological class of partitions in the non-bipartite case. Understanding this topological class of partitions requires the introduction of a modified Laplacian operator, or an equivalent Aharonov-Bohm Hamiltonian with singular magnetic potential. I will also explain how to construct energy-decreasing deformations of partitions that are critical but not minimal, giving insight into the geometric structure of the true minimum. This talk is based on joint work with Gregory Berkolaiko, Yaiza Canzani, Peter Kuchment and Jeremy Marzuola.

proof_stages.label: Definitions of spectral partitions and their energies
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces planar spectral partitions, bipartiteness, nodal domains of Laplacian eigenfunctions, and the max-first-eigenvalue energy whose k-cell minimizers are the objects of study.

proof_stages.label: Global minimal partitions and the bipartite Courant-sharp characterization
proof_stages.summary: After recalling existence and regularity of global minimal k-partitions, the speaker explains that bipartite global minimizers are exactly nodal partitions of Courant-sharp Laplacian eigenfunctions, which leaves most non-bipartite partitions outside ordinary Laplacian theory.

proof_stages.label: Local variational framework for partitions with corners
proof_stages.summary: To define local minima and Morse index, partitions with corners are parameterized by diffeomorphisms of the domain, and a singular-approximation argument shows that even deformations fixing a corner neighborhood recover the full Hessian Morse index.

proof_stages.label: Bipartite Morse-index theorem and local-global equivalence
proof_stages.summary: For bipartite partitions with corners, critical points of the energy are exactly Laplacian nodal partitions and have Morse index n-k, so local minima, Courant-sharp nodal partitions, and global minimal partitions are equivalent.

proof_stages.label: Cut Laplacians and homology of cuts for non-bipartite partitions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines a cut Laplacian with anti-continuity across a cut set, then defines homology of cuts by bipartite symmetric differences and proves that homologous cuts give unitarily equivalent cut Laplacians.

proof_stages.label: Topological minimality in a homology class
proof_stages.summary: Courant-sharp eigenfunctions of a cut Laplacian yield nodal partitions minimizing the spectral energy among all k-partitions whose boundary contains a cut in the same homology class, recovering the bipartite global theorem when the cut is null-homologous.

proof_stages.label: Unified equivalence and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The final synthesis states that, for partitions with corners, zero-index criticality, being a Courant-sharp cut-Laplacian nodal partition, and minimality within the relevant homology class are equivalent, while global non-bipartite minimality remains open in basic cases and asymptotically in the honeycomb conjecture.

examples.engagement_reason: Beyond listing small-k disk and square partitions, he later applies the cut-Laplacian theorem to the disk Mercedes star and identifies the homology class in which it minimizes.

examples.name: Crossing four-partition versus split triple-junction partition
examples.note: Comparing a cross partition with a nearby-looking split triple-junction partition exposes the failure of ordinary diffeomorphic coordinates and leads to the key approximation idea behind the Morse-index result.
examples.engagement_reason: He compares the crossing four-partition with a split triple-junction partition, explaining why no diffeomorphism relates them and how singular diffeomorphisms approximate the split.
